stem: How many group homomorphisms are there from the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) to the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \)? (A homomorphism is determined by the image of a generator, whose order must divide [[m]].)
solution: A homomorphism \( \mathbb{Z}_{m} \to \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) sends the generator to an element \( x \) with \( [[m]]\,x \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \); the number of such \( x \) is \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts all of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \), but not every element of the domain gives a valid target.
This counts all of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), but only elements whose order divides [[m]] work.
This multiplies the two orders, which counts nothing meaningful here.
This is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]],[[n]]) \), not the count of homomorphisms.
This is off by one from \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) \).

stem: Since \( [[mval]] \) divides \( [[nval]] \), how many INJECTIVE group homomorphisms \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) are there? (An injective homomorphism must send a generator of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \) to an element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) of order exactly [[mval]].)
solution: An injective homomorphism's image is a subgroup isomorphic to \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \), so the generator must map to one of the \( \varphi([[mval]]) \) elements of order exactly [[mval]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \): \( \varphi([[mval]]) = p^{a-1}(p-1)q^{b-1}(q-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total count of ALL homomorphisms \( \gcd(m,n) \), most of which are not injective.
This is the index \( [\mathbb{Z}_n:\mathbb{Z}_m] \), not a count of homomorphisms.
This reports \( m \) itself instead of \( \varphi(m) \).
This drops the prime-power factors of \( \varphi(m) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[mval]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[mval]]) \).

stem: Since \( [[nval]] \) divides \( [[mval]] \), how many SURJECTIVE (onto) group homomorphisms \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) are there? (A surjective homomorphism must send a generator of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \) to a GENERATOR of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \).)
solution: A surjective homomorphism's image is all of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), so the generator of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[mval]]} \) must map to one of the \( \varphi([[nval]]) \) generators of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \): \( \varphi([[nval]]) = p^{a-1}(p-1)q^{b-1}(q-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total count of ALL homomorphisms \( \gcd(m,n) \), most of which are not surjective.
This is the index \( [\mathbb{Z}_m:\mathbb{Z}_n] \), not a count of homomorphisms.
This reports \( n \) itself instead of \( \varphi(n) \).
This drops the prime-power factors of \( \varphi(n) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[nval]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[nval]]) \).

stem: How many group homomorphisms are there from the symmetric group \( S_{[[n]]} \) (with \( [[n]] \ge 3 \)) to the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \)? (Every homomorphism from \( S_{[[n]]} \) to an abelian group must factor through the sign map, since the abelianization of \( S_{[[n]]} \) is \( \mathbb{Z}_2 \).)
solution: Every homomorphism \( S_{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \) factors through the abelianization \( S_{[[n]]}^{\text{ab}} \cong \mathbb{Z}_2 \), so the count equals the number of homomorphisms \( \mathbb{Z}_2 \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \), which is \( \gcd(2,[[k]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \) as a valid image of a generator, ignoring that \( S_n \) is nonabelian.
This forgets that the trivial homomorphism is also always available, or double counts when \( k \) is odd.
This wrongly uses \( |S_{[[n]]}| \), which is irrelevant once you pass to the abelianization.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This forgets the extra homomorphism that exists when \( [[k]] \) is even.
